region_of_origin: India (Gujarat); served as street food in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dabeli; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class covering stuffed-bun preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Filled rolls and sandwiches) is the closest defensible category for Dabeli, a spiced-potato-filled pav bun. No exact Dabeli-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dabeli or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, garam masala, chilli powder)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Dabeli is 2 pieces (approximately 250 g), each consisting of a buttered and toasted pav bun filled with spiced mashed potato, roasted peanuts, pomegranate seeds, sev, and chutneys.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — composite estimate for Dabeli based on component ingredients (pav, potato, peanuts, chutneys, sev)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Dabeli as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component entries for white bread roll, boiled potato, roasted peanuts, sev, and standard chutney ingredients, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the same components. The input record values (energy 220 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 11.2 g, carbohydrate 24 g, fibre 3.2 g, sodium 320 mg) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread GI reference and potato GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dabeli as a composite dish. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: white bread roll (GI ~75, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled potato (GI ~65–78, University of Sydney GI Database). The composite GI is weighted toward the pav bun which constitutes the majority of carbohydrate mass. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 60 g carbohydrate in 250 g serving) / 100 = 42.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Dabeli to one piece per occasion, request minimal chutney to reduce added sodium and sugar, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) driven by white pav bun (GI ~75) and boiled potato filling; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 60 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 42.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dabeli is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load; if consumed, portion should be limited to one piece, paired with a protein or fat source to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and chutneys should be minimised to reduce concentrated sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g (from butter and peanuts) and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g; a 250 g serving provides 10 g saturated fat, approaching 50% of the 20 g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Dabeli to one piece per occasion, request dry-toasted pav to eliminate butter-derived saturated fat, and be aware that the sev (fried chickpea noodles) contributes additional fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato and chilli powder are nightshade vegetables containing solanine and capsaicin alkaloids, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Dabeli due to its nightshade content (potato, chilli), monitor individual inflammatory response if consumed, and request a milder preparation with minimal chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dabeli (also known as Kutchi Dabeli or Pav Dabeli) is a popular Indian street food snack originating from the Kutch region of Gujarat, India, and widely enjoyed across India and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of a soft white bread roll (pav) that is buttered and toasted on a griddle, then filled with a spiced mashed potato mixture cooked with a special Dabeli masala (a blend of dried red chillies, coriander, cumin, cinnamon, cloves, and other spices), sweet-tangy tamarind and date chutney, and green coriander chutney. The filling is garnished with roasted peanuts, fresh pomegranate seeds, and sev (crispy fried chickpea-flour noodles), giving the snack a complex interplay of sweet, spicy, tangy, and crunchy textures. A standard serving is 2 pieces (approximately 250 g), providing around 550 kcal, 15 g protein, 28 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. Clinically, Dabeli has a high glycemic index (~70) due to the white pav bun and potato filling, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes unless portion-controlled. Sodium content (~800 mg per serving) warrants caution for hypertension management. Saturated fat from butter and peanuts (~10 g per serving) is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten, peanuts, and dairy.
common_user_queries: Can I eat Dabeli if I have hypertension?
What are the ingredients in Dabeli?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Dabeli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component entries for white bread roll, boiled potato, roasted peanuts, sev, and chutneys, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input record values are consistent with this component-based estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors (white pav bun and potato) from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Dabeli as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level fatty acid data would require a full recipe disaggregation not performed here. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the composite dish is not available in the primary sources consulted, though estimated ~250 mg/100 g from potato and peanut components. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA for this preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for Dabeli as a composite dish is not available in a single approved-source entry; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central do not report these values for this specific preparation. Food subgroup corrected from 'Fried snacks' to 'Stuffed buns and sandwiches' as Dabeli is primarily a stuffed bun preparation, not a fried snack.
